core_story.insight: Explainable AI (XAI) seeks to address this challenge by providing insights into how models arrive at speciﬁc predictions, thus enhancing trust, accountability, and usability in critical applications.

core_story.summary: Introduction Understanding the decision-making process of complex deep neural networks is crucial for their deployment in high-stakes applications such as medical imaging [ 6, 7], autonomous systems [ 2, 43], etc. Introduction Understanding the decision-making process of complex deep neural networks is crucial for their deployment in high-stakes applications such as medical imaging [ 6, 7], autonomous systems [ 2, 43], etc. Explainable AI (XAI) seeks to address this challenge by providing insights into how models arrive at speciﬁc predictions, thus enhancing trust, accountability, and usability in critical applications. Through comprehensive evaluation on both counter- factual explanation and feature attribution we show that our method yields state-of-the-art results for both tasks while preserving the essential properties expected of XAI tools. We demonstrate the effec- tiveness of FreeMCG by applying it to both counterfactual generation and feature attribution, which have tradition- ally been treated as different tasks requiring distinct meth- ods.

problem_expression: Introduction Understanding the decision-making process of complex deep neural networks is crucial for their deployment in high-stakes applications such as medical imaging [ 6, 7], autonomous systems [ 2, 43], etc.
Explainable AI (XAI) seeks to address this challenge by providing insights into how models arrive at speciﬁc predictions, thus enhancing trust, accountability, and usability in critical applications.
limitation_expression: However , they (1) require white-box ac- cess to models, (2) are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, and (3) produce attributions that lie off the image mani- fold, leading to explanations that are not amenable to hu- man perception.

FreeMCG still assumes black-box access to the model’s logits; in more restricted scenarios where only class predictions are available, FreeMCG would not be applicable, highlighting a limitation that could inspire future work on extending FreeMCG to such settings.
method_expression: Through comprehensive evaluation on both counter- factual explanation and feature attribution we show that our method yields state-of-the-art results for both tasks while preserving the essential properties expected of XAI tools.
Explainable AI (XAI) seeks to address this challenge by providing insights into how models arrive at speciﬁc predictions, thus enhancing trust, accountability, and usability in critical applications.

To address these challenges, in this work, we propose Derivative-Free Diffusion Manifold Constrained Gradients (FreeMCG), a uniﬁed framework that can be used both for feature attribution and counterfactual explanation with only black-box access to the target model.
Experiments We demonstrate our method from both feature attribution and counterfactual perspectives.
To evaluate generalization and domain-speciﬁc performance, we compare our method on ImageNet [12] and MIMIC CXR [ 26] dataset, using im- ages with a resolution of 256 ⇥256.
experiment_expression: We demonstrate the effec- tiveness of FreeMCG by applying it to both counterfactual generation and feature attribution, which have tradition- ally been treated as different tasks requiring distinct meth- ods.

Our contributions can be summarized as follows: • We present the ﬁrst uniﬁed framework that encompasses both feature attribution and counterfactual explanation. • We derive a novel method of estimating the gradient of the target classiﬁer with black-box access (i.e. without access to model weights) by leveraging diffusion models and the theory of EnKF. • We demonstrate that FreeMCG achieves state-of-the-art performance, even compared to white-box methods.
Experiments We demonstrate our method from both feature attribution and counterfactual perspectives.
We demonstrate through experiments that FreeMCG enables a uniﬁed framework for both explainabil- ity methods with state-of-the-art results, all without access to model weights.
contribution_expression: Our contributions can be summarized as follows: • We present the ﬁrst uniﬁed framework that encompasses both feature attribution and counterfactual explanation. • We derive a novel method of estimating the gradient of the target classiﬁer with black-box access (i.e. without access to model weights) by leveraging diffusion models and the theory of EnKF. • We demonstrate that FreeMCG achieves state-of-the-art performance, even compared to white-box methods.

Introduction Understanding the decision-making process of complex deep neural networks is crucial for their deployment in high-stakes applications such as medical imaging [ 6, 7], autonomous systems [ 2, 43], etc.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Gradient-based methods are a prototypical family of “ex-
plainability for AI” (XAI) techniques, especially for image-
based models. However , they (1) require white-box ac-
cess to models, (2) are vulnerable to adversarial attacks,
and (3) produce attributions that lie off the image mani-
fold, leading to explanations that are not amenable to hu-
man perception. To overcome these challenges, we intro-
duce Derivative-Free Diffusion Manifold-Contrained Gra-
dients (FreeMCG): by leveraging ensemble Kalman ﬁl-
ters and diffusion models, we derive a derivative-free ap-
proximation of the model’s gradient projected onto the
data manifold , requiring access only to the model’s out-
puts (i.e., black-box setting). We demonstrate the effec-
tiveness of FreeMCG by applying it to both counterfactual
generation and feature attribution, which have tradition-
ally been treated as different tasks requiring distinct meth-
ods. Through comprehensive evaluation on both counter-
factual explanation and feature attribution we show that our
method yields state-of-the-art results for both tasks while
preserving the essential properties expected of XAI tools.
Code: https://github.com/one-june/FreeMCG.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Understanding the decision-making process of complex
deep neural networks is crucial for their deployment in
high-stakes applications such as medical imaging [ 6, 7],
autonomous systems [ 2, 43], etc. Explainable AI (XAI)
seeks to address this challenge by providing insights into
how models arrive at speciﬁc predictions, thus enhancing
trust, accountability, and usability in critical applications.
Two widely used tools for XAI are counterfactual explana-
tion [59] and feature attribution 1 [31].
Among various XAI methods, the gradient of the target
(e.g. classiﬁer) model is the prototypical method of under-
*Equal contribution
1The term “saliency maps” are also often used interchangeably.
CounterfactualSource
Class: “Tiger”
Class: “Cheetah”

Figure 1. Representative results of FreeMCG, operating as a uni-
versal framework for black box XAI.
standing the local behavior of a function around a given
input data point. It has thus been used since the begin-
ning of the rise of neural networks to explain their deci-
sions [ 50]. There have been many subsequent works im-
proving the vanilla gradient to serve as better feature attri-
butions for a deep neural network [ 3, 46, 47, 55, 57, 61].
Gradients are also utilized for generating counterfactual ex-
planations [ 4, 25, 44], in which the gradient of the target
model serves as the direction that guides the change � made
to the original image so that prediction of the model ﬂips to
another class.
While gradient-based methods are popular explainable
AI (XAI) tools, they suffer from three signiﬁcant limita-
tions:
raw_sections.method:
3. Lack of a uniﬁed framework for feature attribution
and counterfactual explanations: The literature on fea-
ture attribution and counterfactual explanations remains
largely separate. This is because real gradients produce
adversarial attacks rather than counterfactual explana-
tions and additional techniques are required to introduce
perceptible changes. In theory, gradients should be us-
able for both applications. This raises the question of
whether a single framework can effectively handle both
types of explanations.
To address these challenges, in this work, we propose
Derivative-Free Diffusion Manifold Constrained Gradients
(FreeMCG), a uniﬁed framework that can be used both for
feature attribution and counterfactual explanation with only
black-box access to the target model. By leveraging diffu-
sion models and their powerful ability to model data man-
ifolds, we can produce on-manifold perturbations of input
data points, which, combined with ideas from Ensemble
Kalman Filters (EnKF) [ 45], can be used to approximate
manifold-projected model gradients. In Fig. 1, we show
the representative results of FreeMCG, acting as a uniﬁed
framework for XAI. Our contributions can be summarized
as follows:
• We present the ﬁrst uniﬁed framework that encompasses
both feature attribution and counterfactual explanation.
• We derive a novel method of estimating the gradient of
the target classiﬁer with black-box access (i.e. without
access to model weights) by leveraging diffusion models
and the theory of EnKF.
• We demonstrate that FreeMCG achieves state-of-the-art
performance, even compared to white-box methods.
raw_sections.experiments:
4. Experiments
We demonstrate our method from both feature attribution
and counterfactual perspectives. To evaluate generalization
and domain-speciﬁc performance, we compare our method
on ImageNet [12] and MIMIC CXR [ 26] dataset, using im-
ages with a resolution of 256 ⇥256. We use regular pre-
trained classiﬁer models with the ResNet-50 [ 20] and un-
conditional diffusion models [ 13] trained on each dataset.
Experimental details are provided in Appendix H.
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusion
We developed Derivative-Free Diffusion Mainfold Con-
strained Gradients ( FreeMCG), an approximation of a neu-
ral network’s gradient tangent to the data manifold, ob-
tained without access to the model weights or its real gra-
dients. Previous explainability literature on gradient-based
feature attribution and counterfactual generation have re-
mained largely separate because the gradient could not be
readily employed for counterfactual generation as it pro-
duces adversarial attacks. With FreeMCG, one can obtain
gradient directions on the image manifold that produce se-
mantically meaningful counterfactual explanations and fea-
ture attributions. We demonstrate through experiments that
FreeMCG enables a uniﬁed framework for both explainabil-
ity methods with state-of-the-art results, all without access
to model weights. FreeMCG still assumes black-box access
to the model’s logits; in more restricted scenarios where
only class predictions are available, FreeMCG would not
be applicable, highlighting a limitation that could inspire
future work on extending FreeMCG to such settings.
